If they indicate a grounded blocker, should the plan reroute immediately, and only fall back to clearing if rerouting is not grounded or has already failed?\n    6. If a key item is missing from the current facts, the plan must explicitly commit to one concrete acquisition route supported by the current facts or immediate history.\n       You must name the missing item, the evidence that it is missing, and a concrete place, object, menu, or exit that the plan is moving toward.\n       Do not use vague phrases like \"obtain X\" or \"go get X\" without naming a concrete route.\n       If an acquisition hint is provided above, treat it only as a soft hint and follow it only when it is consistent with the current facts.\n    7. If this is an explicit crop-growth or once-per-day farming task, do any one-per-day constraints matter today, and should the plan switch to waiting or sleep positioning instead? Otherwise skip crop-growth logic entirely.\n    7a. If this is a navigation, exploration, or quest task, what is the concrete next waypoint right now: exit, door, building, floor transition, quest board, or NPC?\n    7b. If this is a social or shopping task, what concrete interaction context matters right now: clerk counter, blacksmith counter, carpenter menu, shipping bin, gift target NPC, or dialogue/menu state?\n    7c. If this is a crafting task, what current facts support craft(item): recipe/material readiness, crafting menu access, or a concrete route to missing materials?\n    7d. If this is a combat task, where is the enemy relative to the player, is the correct weapon selected, and is the next productive step to close distance, re-face, or attack?\n    7e. If this is a clear_* debris-clearing task and no matching obstacle is visible right now, what is the shortest grounded search move toward uncleared farm interior or a visibly cluttered area? Do not anchor the plan to decorative trees, flowers, bushes, speculative remote landmarks, or roof-occluded space behind a building unless the matching debris is explicitly visible there.\n    8. Check the latest execution feedback and task progress signal. If productive actions made no progress for 2 or more consecutive steps, or if the feedback says the player is not adjacent / in the wrong position, the first planned action should usually be move(...). If the current facts show a clearable obstacle directly in front of the player or on the current effect point, reroute first unless the route is not grounded or has already failed.\n    9. If the latest action caused no progress, no position change, or a position mismatch, do not begin by repeating the same move or the same-direction use/interact. Reposition, resolve alignment, or switch routes first; only clear the blocking obstacle when rerouting is not grounded or has already failed.\n    9a. ANTI-OSCILLATION: If your last 3+ moves alternate between opposite directions (e.g. move left then right, or move up then down), you are oscillating in place with zero net progress. To break out: (1) commit to ONE direction and move at least 3-5 tiles before reconsidering, (2) only reverse direction if that move was blocked, not because you changed your mind about the target, (3) if you are unsure which direction is correct, pick the direction toward the nearest exit or building listed in Exits/Buildings above and stick with it.\n    10. AXIS ORDER FOR MOVEMENT: When a move needs both x and y displacement AND the Surroundings show an obstacle on one axis, split into two single-axis moves with the clear axis first.\n        - Example: target is at relative (3, -2). If Surroundings show [1, 0]: Stone (right is blocked), move vertically first: move(x=0, y=-2), then move(x=3, y=0).\n        - If no obstacle is visible on either axis, a single diagonal move like move(x=3, y=-2) is fine - the pathfinder will handle it.\n        - Only split when you have concrete evidence of a blocking obstacle from Surroundings.\n    11. Plan 4 sequential actions that maximize concrete progress in this 4-step budget. Prefer productive chains like use -> use -> move -> use or move -> use -> use -> use. Keep move() only when it clearly improves access to a target or a nearby cluster, except when the latest feedback explicitly requires repositioning first. Step 1 must be immediately executable from the CURRENT state.\n    12. Before finalizing, check every planned action against the CURRENT game information again. If any move(), choose_item(slot_index=...), use(direction=\"...\"), or interact(direction=\"...\") is not supported by the current environment facts, replace it with a better grounded action. Also confirm every selected action exists in the valid action set. If your preferred Step 1 is invalid, replace it with the best grounded fallback instead of leaving recovery to later steps.\n\n\n\nActions: Plan a sequence of exactly 4 actions to make progress on the task. These 4 actions should form a coherent multi-step plan. Rules:\n    1. You should output actions in Python code format and specify any necessary parameters to execute that action. If the function has parameters, you should also include their names and decide their values, like \"move(x=3, y=-2)\". If it does not have a parameter, just output the action, like \"unattach_item()\".\n       IMPORTANT: move(x, y) takes RELATIVE offsets from the current position, NOT absolute map coordinates. For example, if you are at position (10, 20) and want to move to (13, 18), output move(x=3, y=-2). Do NOT output move(x=13, y=18). Typical safe range is -15 to 15 per axis. When both axes are needed, movement order matters: choose the axis order that avoids known nearby obstacles, and split the route into separate single-axis moves when that is clearer.\n    2. You MUST output exactly 4 actions as a numbered sequence. NEVER output an empty action list. Every step must contain a concrete action from the valid action set. If you are unsure, choose the safest grounded action supported by the current facts. Do NOT fill the plan with a blind tiny move such as move(x=0, y=1). Step 1 must be a real executable action, not a placeholder for future replanning.\n    3. The 4 actions should be logically sequential and should maximize concrete task progress within only 4 steps.\n    4. Treat move() as somewhat expensive, but still often necessary. Include move() only when it directly enables the next productive action, improves access to a nearby target cluster, or sets up multiple later productive actions.\n    5. If the correct tool is already selected in the toolbar or Item in your hand, do NOT use choose_item(slot_index=...) again. Use choose_item(slot_index=...) only when the current tool is wrong and the switch is necessary for the immediate next real action.\n    6. If a target is already reachable from the current tile, usually start with use() or interact() instead of moving first. However, keep a short repositioning move if it creates a clearly better multi-target position.\n    7. When clearing weeds, stones, wood, or similar nearby obstacles, prefer moving adjacent to a small cluster so the remaining steps can be spent on consecutive productive actions.\n    7a. For clear_* tasks, if no matching obstacle is currently supported by Surroundings or the screenshot, do not add speculative use() swings and do not make long search jumps. Use short search moves, typically 1-3 tiles, that move away from exits/path edges and toward uncleared farm interior or a visibly cluttered area.\n    8. If you want to plant a seed, apply fertilizer, or harvest a mature crop, please use interact(direction=\"...\"). If you want to use tools, like axe, hoe, watering can, pickaxe and scythe, please use use(direction=\"...\"). Remember: seeds and fertilizers (Basic Retaining Soil, Speed-Gro, etc.) are items, NOT tools. In the underlying game, pressing C (use item / left click) or X (check / interact / right click) can both place seeds and fertilizer successfully, but in this action API you should represent seed/fertilizer placement with interact(direction=\"...\") for consistency.\n    8a. If the currently selected item is a seed or fertilizer, do not treat it like a tool swing. Your productive world action with that selected item should normally be interact(direction=\"...\") on a valid nearby target, plus short reposition moves to line up with that target.\n    8b. For seed/fertilizer placement tasks, a valid target must be explicitly supported as HoeDirt / tilled soil. A tile described only as empty is NOT a valid placement target.\n    8c. For sowing, the target must be explicit empty HoeDirt / tilled soil. For fertilizer placement, the target must be explicit unfertilized HoeDirt / tilled soil. It may be empty or may already contain a planted seed or crop, but do NOT target a tile already described as fertilized, Speed-Gro, Retaining Soil, or similar.\n    9. Repeating use() is allowed when it continues making progress on nearby targets. Repeating non-productive setup actions is not.\n    10. Your actions must strictly follow the reasoning and only use actions from the valid action set. Core actions include move(x, y), use(direction=\"...\"), interact(direction=\"...\"), choose_item(slot_index=...), attach_item(slot_index=...), unattach_item(), craft(item), choose_option(option_index, quantity, direction), menu(option, menu_name), and nop(). Never invent actions that are not in the valid action set.\n    11. You MUST prioritize the latest current environment facts over stale history. If the current observation shows that a previous assumption is outdated, do not preserve that assumption in the plan.\n    12. Do not output a tool switch, movement, or interaction unless it is justified by the current menu state, selected item, effect point, surroundings, or another explicit current observation.\n    13. Do not plan toward distant or speculative targets when the current environment already supports a more concrete nearby action.\n    13a. For clear_* tasks, a decorative landmark is not a valid target by itself. Do not write plans like \"go near the pink blossom tree to search for stones\" unless the current facts actually show matching debris there.\n    13b. For farm clearup near the farmhouse, porch, mailbox, barn, or silo, prefer visible walkable debris on open ground. Do not route behind house roofs or other occluded building space unless the current facts explicitly show the target debris there.\n    14. If the latest feedback says the last productive action had no effect, do NOT repeat the same use()/interact() direction as Step 1. Reposition first.\n    14a. For seed/fertilizer placement tasks, reposition locally after failure. Stay near the same farm patch and line up with another explicit HoeDirt target. For sowing, that target must be empty. For fertilizer, it may be empty or already occupied, but it must not already be described as fertilized, Speed-Gro, Retaining Soil, or similar. Do not wander several tiles away from the current farm patch unless the current facts explicitly show a better nearby unfertilized or untilled target elsewhere.\n    14b. If a move just failed with no position change, do not repeat another long move on the same axis as Step 1. First use a short local realignment move (1-2 tiles on the perpendicular axis) to sidestep the obstacle, then retry the original direction with a smaller magnitude. If multiple recent moves on the same axis have all failed, you are likely against a wall or large obstacle - switch to the perpendicular axis for several tiles before retrying.\n    14c. If the visible surroundings already show an obstacle somewhere along the first few tiles of a proposed long move (for example a weed, stone, wall, building, or door on that line), do NOT keep the long move. Shorten the move so it stops before the blocker, or reroute around it first.\n    15. If acquisition is needed, do not replace a concrete route with unexplained porch movement or speculative wandering. Each planned move should visibly advance toward a named place, object, menu, or exit.\n    16. If Front tile summary or Blocked recovery hint shows a concrete blocker directly in front of the player, do not repeat the same-direction wall-bump move. If it is clearable, still prefer rerouting or realigning first; only switch tools and clear it when rerouting is not grounded or has already failed. Use Pickaxe for stone, Axe for wood/twig/log, and Scythe for weeds/grass/fiber.\n    17. NAVIGATION: If the task requires going to a different location (go_to_*, purchase_*, sell_*, forage_*, quest_*), check \"Exits on current map\" above. Each exit shows a name and relative position. Move toward the exit that leads to the target location. Stardew map connections: Farm exits to Bus Stop (east) and Backwoods (north via the pet bowl entrance path); Bus Stop exits to Town (east), Farm (west), Backwoods (north); Town has Pierre's Store, Fish Shop, Blacksmith, etc. Plan your route through map exits step by step.\n    17f. If Exits, Buildings, and Nearest grounded target summary do NOT provide a precise waypoint, do NOT invent a long jump based on generic map knowledge. If a same-task Long-term memory reference provides a successful route prefix, follow that short prefix for the next planning horizon; otherwise use a short grounded probe move of 1-3 tiles that clearly improves alignment, then re-observe.\n    17a. BED / SLEEP: To go to bed, follow these steps in order:\n        (1) If you are OUTSIDE the farmhouse: navigate to the farmhouse entrance on the SOUTH side. Check Buildings above for the farmhouse position.\n        (2) When you are directly in front of the door (Surroundings shows \"Farmhouse Door\" or \"Door, exit:\" at [0, -1]): use the TWO-STEP door entry:\n            Step A: move(x=0, y=-1) - this will be BLOCKED by the door, but it makes your character FACE the door (facing direction is required).\n            Step B: interact(direction=\"up\") - now that you face the door, this opens and enters it.\n            Both steps are required. A bare interact() without the facing move will fail with \"no confirmation\".\n        (3) Once INSIDE the farmhouse (Location shows \"FarmHouse\"): the bed is in the lower-right corner. Move right and down using move() commands.\n        (4) Walk ONTO the bed tile using move(). The bed IS walkable - you can step onto it.\n        (5) Once you are standing ON the bed tile, use interact() to trigger sleep. This is required - simply standing on the bed is not enough.\n    17b. ENTERING BUILDINGS: ALL building doors (farmhouse, coop, barn, store) require the TWO-STEP door entry:\n        Step A: move(x=0, y=-1) (or direction toward door) to FACE the door. The move will be blocked but the facing updates.\n        Step B: interact(direction=<toward door>) to actually open/enter.\n        Do NOT call interact() alone without the facing move - the character may be facing a different direction and interact() will fail.\n        For the Coop: typically located west of the farmhouse.\n    17c. NEAR MAP EXITS: When you are within ~5 tiles of a map exit (check Exits above), switch to unit moves of exactly 1 tile: move(x=1, y=0), move(x=0, y=-1), etc. Map exits are often narrow paths (1-2 tiles wide) that require precise navigation. Large moves will overshoot or hit the boundary.\n    17d. STUCK-MOVE ESCALATION: If 2 or more consecutive move() calls on the same axis have failed (blocked), do NOT repeat the same direction or magnitude. Instead: (1) try a perpendicular axis first with a short move of 1-2 tiles to sidestep the obstacle, then resume the original direction; (2) if the perpendicular move also fails, try the opposite perpendicular direction; (3) reduce move magnitude to 1 tile per step until you find an unblocked path. Never repeat a blocked large move (e.g. move(x=10, y=0)) - always drop to unit moves and probe around obstacles.\n    17e. CLEAR OBSTACLES DURING NAVIGATION: If you are blocked and Surroundings shows a clearable obstacle on the blocked tile (Stone, Twig, Wood, Weeds, Grass, Fiber), you have the tools to remove it. Switch to the matching tool (Pickaxe for Stone, Axe for Twig/Wood, Scythe for Weeds/Grass/Fiber) using choose_item(), then use(direction=<toward obstacle>) to clear it. After clearing, resume navigation. This is faster than trying to route around when no clear path exists.\n    18. SOCIAL / SHOPPING: For sell_*, purchase_*, talk_*, gift_*, upgrade_*, break_* geode, or quest interactions, prioritize reaching the correct NPC, shop counter, service desk, or interaction menu. If you are already aligned at the correct counter or NPC, prefer interact(...), choose_option(...), or menu(...) over wandering.\n19. CRAFTING: For direct craft_* tasks, craft(item) works directly and should be preferred once recipe/material readiness is supported by the current facts. Do not waste steps opening the crafting menu first unless the current menu facts already show a crafting context. If materials are missing, the plan should move toward a concrete source instead of falling back to crop-growth logic.\n19a. For crafting, follow the exact missing recipe materials supported by the current facts. Do not substitute a nearby but wrong material source (for example, do not route to Fiber when the recipe is missing Sap).\n    20. COMBAT: For kill_* tasks, prioritize closing distance to the target enemy, keeping the weapon selected, facing the enemy, and using the weapon when the enemy is adjacent or clearly in attack range. Do not replace combat plans with farming, watering, or shopping actions.\n    20a. If a kill_* task has no visible enemy in the current facts, do NOT make long blind sweeps or oscillating cardinal moves across the floor. Use short grounded mine-search moves, typically 1-3 tiles, toward visible open corridors, room openings, or unexplored edges near the current floor instead of random long traversals.\n    20b. If a kill_* task already has a visible enemy on an adjacent tile, on the facing line, or explicitly described as close to the player, do NOT keep searching. If Rusty Sword is already selected, Step 1 should usually be use(direction=\"...\") to attack, or a one-tile local alignment move only when the current facing is clearly wrong.\n    21. When navigating between maps, your 4 actions should focus on reaching the nearest relevant exit. Do NOT waste steps on non-navigation actions when the target is on a different map.\n\n\n\nYou should only respond in the format described below, and you should not output comments or other information.\nReasoning:\n1. ...\n2. ...\n3. ...\nActions:\n```python\n    # Step 1\n    move(x=3, y=-2)\n    # Step 2\n    choose_item(slot_index=0)\n    # Step 3\n    use(direction=\"right\")\n    # Step 4\n    use(direction=\"down\")\n```"
            }
        ]
    }
]

Then select a new one, otherwise reuse the last subtask.\n13. If you propose a new subtask, explain briefly why it is more feasible for the current situation.\n14. The proposed subtask must be one precise sentence, grounded in the current facts, and must not mention skills or grid information.\n15. Do not use vague phrases like \"obtain X\" or \"go somewhere\". Name the concrete place, object, menu, route, exit, NPC, enemy, or material source.\n\n\n\nYou should only respond in the format described below, and you should not output comments or other information.\nHistory_summary:\nThe summary is...\nSubtask_reasoning:\n1. ...\n2. ...\n...\nSubtask:\nThe current subtask is"
            }
        ]
    }
]
````

If a key item is missing from the current facts, the plan must explicitly commit to one concrete acquisition route supported by the current facts or immediate history.\n       You must name the missing item, the evidence that it is missing, and a concrete place, object, menu, or exit that the plan is moving toward.\n       Do not use vague phrases like \"obtain X\" or \"go get X\" without naming a concrete route.\n       If an acquisition hint is provided above, treat it only as a soft hint and follow it only when it is consistent with the current facts.\n    7. If this is an explicit crop-growth or once-per-day farming task, do any one-per-day constraints matter today, and should the plan switch to waiting or sleep positioning instead? Otherwise skip crop-growth logic entirely.\n    7a. If this is a navigation, exploration, or quest task, what is the concrete next waypoint right now: exit, door, building, floor transition, quest board, or NPC?\n    7b. If this is a social or shopping task, what concrete interaction context matters right now: clerk counter, blacksmith counter, carpenter menu, shipping bin, gift target NPC, or dialogue/menu state?\n    7c. If this is a crafting task, what current facts support craft(item): recipe/material readiness, crafting menu access, or a concrete route to missing materials?\n    7d. If this is a combat task, where is the enemy relative to the player, is the correct weapon selected, and is the next productive step to close distance, re-face, or attack?\n    7e. If this is a clear_* debris-clearing task and no matching obstacle is visible right now, what is the shortest grounded search move toward uncleared farm interior or a visibly cluttered area? Do not anchor the plan to decorative trees, flowers, bushes, speculative remote landmarks, or roof-occluded space behind a building unless the matching debris is explicitly visible there.\n    8. Check the latest execution feedback and task progress signal. If productive actions made no progress for 2 or more consecutive steps, or if the feedback says the player is not adjacent / in the wrong position, the first planned action should usually be move(...). If the current facts show a clearable obstacle directly in front of the player or on the current effect point, reroute first unless the route is not grounded or has already failed.\n    9. If the latest action caused no progress, no position change, or a position mismatch, do not begin by repeating the same move or the same-direction use/interact. Reposition, resolve alignment, or switch routes first; only clear the blocking obstacle when rerouting is not grounded or has already failed.\n    9a. ANTI-OSCILLATION: If your last 3+ moves alternate between opposite directions (e.g. move left then right, or move up then down), you are oscillating in place with zero net progress. To break out: (1) commit to ONE direction and move at least 3-5 tiles before reconsidering, (2) only reverse direction if that move was blocked, not because you changed your mind about the target, (3) if you are unsure which direction is correct, pick the direction toward the nearest exit or building listed in Exits/Buildings above and stick with it.\n    10. AXIS ORDER FOR MOVEMENT: When a move needs both x and y displacement AND the Surroundings show an obstacle on one axis, split into two single-axis moves with the clear axis first.\n        - Example: target is at relative (3, -2). If Surroundings show [1, 0]: Stone (right is blocked), move vertically first: move(x=0, y=-2), then move(x=3, y=0).\n        - If no obstacle is visible on either axis, a single diagonal move like move(x=3, y=-2) is fine - the pathfinder will handle it.\n        - Only split when you have concrete evidence of a blocking obstacle from Surroundings.\n    11. Plan 4 sequential actions that maximize concrete progress in this 4-step budget. Prefer productive chains like use -> use -> move -> use or move -> use -> use -> use. Keep move() only when it clearly improves access to a target or a nearby cluster, except when the latest feedback explicitly requires repositioning first. Step 1 must be immediately executable from the CURRENT state.\n    12. Before finalizing, check every planned action against the CURRENT game information again. If any move(), choose_item(slot_index=...), use(direction=\"...\"), or interact(direction=\"...\") is not supported by the current environment facts, replace it with a better grounded action. Also confirm every selected action exists in the valid action set. If your preferred Step 1 is invalid, replace it with the best grounded fallback instead of leaving recovery to later steps.\n\n\n\nActions: Plan a sequence of exactly 4 actions to make progress on the task. These 4 actions should form a coherent multi-step plan. Rules:\n    1. You should output actions in Python code format and specify any necessary parameters to execute that action. If the function has parameters, you should also include their names and decide their values, like \"move(x=3, y=-2)\". If it does not have a parameter, just output the action, like \"unattach_item()\".\n       IMPORTANT: move(x, y) takes RELATIVE offsets from the current position, NOT absolute map coordinates. For example, if you are at position (10, 20) and want to move to (13, 18), output move(x=3, y=-2). Do NOT output move(x=13, y=18). Typical safe range is -15 to 15 per axis. When both axes are needed, movement order matters: choose the axis order that avoids known nearby obstacles, and split the route into separate single-axis moves when that is clearer.\n    2. You MUST output exactly 4 actions as a numbered sequence. NEVER output an empty action list. Every step must contain a concrete action from the valid action set. If you are unsure, choose the safest grounded action supported by the current facts. Do NOT fill the plan with a blind tiny move such as move(x=0, y=1). Step 1 must be a real executable action, not a placeholder for future replanning.\n    3. The 4 actions should be logically sequential and should maximize concrete task progress within only 4 steps.\n    4. Treat move() as somewhat expensive, but still often necessary. Include move() only when it directly enables the next productive action, improves access to a nearby target cluster, or sets up multiple later productive actions.\n    5. If the correct tool is already selected in the toolbar or Item in your hand, do NOT use choose_item(slot_index=...) again. Use choose_item(slot_index=...) only when the current tool is wrong and the switch is necessary for the immediate next real action.\n    6. If a target is already reachable from the current tile, usually start with use() or interact() instead of moving first. However, keep a short repositioning move if it creates a clearly better multi-target position.\n    7. When clearing weeds, stones, wood, or similar nearby obstacles, prefer moving adjacent to a small cluster so the remaining steps can be spent on consecutive productive actions.\n    7a. For clear_* tasks, if no matching obstacle is currently supported by Surroundings or the screenshot, do not add speculative use() swings and do not make long search jumps. Use short search moves, typically 1-3 tiles, that move away from exits/path edges and toward uncleared farm interior or a visibly cluttered area.\n    8. If you want to plant a seed, apply fertilizer, or harvest a mature crop, please use interact(direction=\"...\"). If you want to use tools, like axe, hoe, watering can, pickaxe and scythe, please use use(direction=\"...\"). Remember: seeds and fertilizers (Basic Retaining Soil, Speed-Gro, etc.) are items, NOT tools. In the underlying game, pressing C (use item / left click) or X (check / interact / right click) can both place seeds and fertilizer successfully, but in this action API you should represent seed/fertilizer placement with interact(direction=\"...\") for consistency.\n    8a. If the currently selected item is a seed or fertilizer, do not treat it like a tool swing. Your productive world action with that selected item should normally be interact(direction=\"...\") on a valid nearby target, plus short reposition moves to line up with that target.\n    8b. For seed/fertilizer placement tasks, a valid target must be explicitly supported as HoeDirt / tilled soil. A tile described only as empty is NOT a valid placement target.\n    8c. For sowing, the target must be explicit empty HoeDirt / tilled soil. For fertilizer placement, the target must be explicit unfertilized HoeDirt / tilled soil. It may be empty or may already contain a planted seed or crop, but do NOT target a tile already described as fertilized, Speed-Gro, Retaining Soil, or similar.\n    9. Repeating use() is allowed when it continues making progress on nearby targets. Repeating non-productive setup actions is not.\n    10. Your actions must strictly follow the reasoning and only use actions from the valid action set. Core actions include move(x, y), use(direction=\"...\"), interact(direction=\"...\"), choose_item(slot_index=...), attach_item(slot_index=...), unattach_item(), craft(item), choose_option(option_index, quantity, direction), menu(option, menu_name), and nop(). Never invent actions that are not in the valid action set.\n    11. You MUST prioritize the latest current environment facts over stale history. If the current observation shows that a previous assumption is outdated, do not preserve that assumption in the plan.\n    12. Do not output a tool switch, movement, or interaction unless it is justified by the current menu state, selected item, effect point, surroundings, or another explicit current observation.\n    13. Do not plan toward distant or speculative targets when the current environment already supports a more concrete nearby action.\n    13a. For clear_* tasks, a decorative landmark is not a valid target by itself. Do not write plans like \"go near the pink blossom tree to search for stones\" unless the current facts actually show matching debris there.\n    13b. For farm clearup near the farmhouse, porch, mailbox, barn, or silo, prefer visible walkable debris on open ground. Do not route behind house roofs or other occluded building space unless the current facts explicitly show the target debris there.\n    14. If the latest feedback says the last productive action had no effect, do NOT repeat the same use()/interact() direction as Step 1. Reposition first.\n    14a. For seed/fertilizer placement tasks, reposition locally after failure. Stay near the same farm patch and line up with another explicit HoeDirt target. For sowing, that target must be empty. For fertilizer, it may be empty or already occupied, but it must not already be described as fertilized, Speed-Gro, Retaining Soil, or similar. Do not wander several tiles away from the current farm patch unless the current facts explicitly show a better nearby unfertilized or untilled target elsewhere.\n    14b. If a move just failed with no position change, do not repeat another long move on the same axis as Step 1. First use a short local realignment move (1-2 tiles on the perpendicular axis) to sidestep the obstacle, then retry the original direction with a smaller magnitude. If multiple recent moves on the same axis have all failed, you are likely against a wall or large obstacle - switch to the perpendicular axis for several tiles before retrying.\n    14c. If the visible surroundings already show an obstacle somewhere along the first few tiles of a proposed long move (for example a weed, stone, wall, building, or door on that line), do NOT keep the long move. Shorten the move so it stops before the blocker, or reroute around it first.\n    15. If acquisition is needed, do not replace a concrete route with unexplained porch movement or speculative wandering. Each planned move should visibly advance toward a named place, object, menu, or exit.\n    16. If Front tile summary or Blocked recovery hint shows a concrete blocker directly in front of the player, do not repeat the same-direction wall-bump move. If it is clearable, still prefer rerouting or realigning first; only switch tools and clear it when rerouting is not grounded or has already failed. Use Pickaxe for stone, Axe for wood/twig/log, and Scythe for weeds/grass/fiber.\n    17. NAVIGATION: If the task requires going to a different location (go_to_*, purchase_*, sell_*, forage_*, quest_*), check \"Exits on current map\" above. Each exit shows a name and relative position. Move toward the exit that leads to the target location. Stardew map connections: Farm exits to Bus Stop (east) and Backwoods (north via the pet bowl entrance path); Bus Stop exits to Town (east), Farm (west), Backwoods (north); Town has Pierre's Store, Fish Shop, Blacksmith, etc. Plan your route through map exits step by step.\n    17f. If Exits, Buildings, and Nearest grounded target summary do NOT provide a precise waypoint, do NOT invent a long jump based on generic map knowledge. If a same-task Long-term memory reference provides a successful route prefix, follow that short prefix for the next planning horizon; otherwise use a short grounded probe move of 1-3 tiles that clearly improves alignment, then re-observe.\n    17a. BED / SLEEP: To go to bed, follow these steps in order:\n        (1) If you are OUTSIDE the farmhouse: navigate to the farmhouse entrance on the SOUTH side. Check Buildings above for the farmhouse position.\n        (2) When you are directly in front of the door (Surroundings shows \"Farmhouse Door\" or \"Door, exit:\" at [0, -1]): use the TWO-STEP door entry:\n            Step A: move(x=0, y=-1) - this will be BLOCKED by the door, but it makes your character FACE the door (facing direction is required).\n            Step B: interact(direction=\"up\") - now that you face the door, this opens and enters it.\n            Both steps are required. A bare interact() without the facing move will fail with \"no confirmation\".\n        (3) Once INSIDE the farmhouse (Location shows \"FarmHouse\"): the bed is in the lower-right corner. Move right and down using move() commands.\n        (4) Walk ONTO the bed tile using move(). The bed IS walkable - you can step onto it.\n        (5) Once you are standing ON the bed tile, use interact() to trigger sleep. This is required - simply standing on the bed is not enough.\n    17b. ENTERING BUILDINGS: ALL building doors (farmhouse, coop, barn, store) require the TWO-STEP door entry:\n        Step A: move(x=0, y=-1) (or direction toward door) to FACE the door. The move will be blocked but the facing updates.\n        Step B: interact(direction=<toward door>) to actually open/enter.\n        Do NOT call interact() alone without the facing move - the character may be facing a different direction and interact() will fail.\n        For the Coop: typically located west of the farmhouse.\n    17c. NEAR MAP EXITS: When you are within ~5 tiles of a map exit (check Exits above), switch to unit moves of exactly 1 tile: move(x=1, y=0), move(x=0, y=-1), etc. Map exits are often narrow paths (1-2 tiles wide) that require precise navigation. Large moves will overshoot or hit the boundary.\n    17d. STUCK-MOVE ESCALATION: If 2 or more consecutive move() calls on the same axis have failed (blocked), do NOT repeat the same direction or magnitude. Instead: (1) try a perpendicular axis first with a short move of 1-2 tiles to sidestep the obstacle, then resume the original direction; (2) if the perpendicular move also fails, try the opposite perpendicular direction; (3) reduce move magnitude to 1 tile per step until you find an unblocked path. Never repeat a blocked large move (e.g. move(x=10, y=0)) - always drop to unit moves and probe around obstacles.\n    17e. CLEAR OBSTACLES DURING NAVIGATION: If you are blocked and Surroundings shows a clearable obstacle on the blocked tile (Stone, Twig, Wood, Weeds, Grass, Fiber), you have the tools to remove it. Switch to the matching tool (Pickaxe for Stone, Axe for Twig/Wood, Scythe for Weeds/Grass/Fiber) using choose_item(), then use(direction=<toward obstacle>) to clear it. After clearing, resume navigation. This is faster than trying to route around when no clear path exists.\n    18. SOCIAL / SHOPPING: For sell_*, purchase_*, talk_*, gift_*, upgrade_*, break_* geode, or quest interactions, prioritize reaching the correct NPC, shop counter, service desk, or interaction menu. If you are already aligned at the correct counter or NPC, prefer interact(...), choose_option(...), or menu(...) over wandering.\n19. CRAFTING: For direct craft_* tasks, craft(item) works directly and should be preferred once recipe/material readiness is supported by the current facts. Do not waste steps opening the crafting menu first unless the current menu facts already show a crafting context. If materials are missing, the plan should move toward a concrete source instead of falling back to crop-growth logic.\n19a. For crafting, follow the exact missing recipe materials supported by the current facts. Do not substitute a nearby but wrong material source (for example, do not route to Fiber when the recipe is missing Sap).\n    20. COMBAT: For kill_* tasks, prioritize closing distance to the target enemy, keeping the weapon selected, facing the enemy, and using the weapon when the enemy is adjacent or clearly in attack range. Do not replace combat plans with farming, watering, or shopping actions.\n    20a. If a kill_* task has no visible enemy in the current facts, do NOT make long blind sweeps or oscillating cardinal moves across the floor. Use short grounded mine-search moves, typically 1-3 tiles, toward visible open corridors, room openings, or unexplored edges near the current floor instead of random long traversals.\n    20b. If a kill_* task already has a visible enemy on an adjacent tile, on the facing line, or explicitly described as close to the player, do NOT keep searching. If Rusty Sword is already selected, Step 1 should usually be use(direction=\"...\") to attack, or a one-tile local alignment move only when the current facing is clearly wrong.\n    21. When navigating between maps, your 4 actions should focus on reaching the nearest relevant exit. Do NOT waste steps on non-navigation actions when the target is on a different map.\n\n\n\nYou should only respond in the format described below, and you should not output comments or other information.\nReasoning:\n1. ...\n2. ...\n3. ...\nActions:\n```python\n    # Step 1\n    move(x=3, y=-2)\n    # Step 2\n    choose_item(slot_index=0)\n    # Step 3\n    use(direction=\"right\")\n    # Step 4\n    use(direction=\"down\")\n```"
            }
        ]
    }
]
